mnemonics.title: Critical Section Requirements
mnemonics.content: MPB: MUTUAL EXCLUSION, PROGRESS, BOUNDED WAITING - MEMORIZE THESE THREE FOR ANY SOLUTION

mnemonics.title: Peterson's Solution Variables
mnemonics.content: TURN AND FLAGS: TURN DETERMINES WHO GOES, FLAGS SHOW WHO WANTS - BOTH NEEDED FOR SYNC

mnemonics.title: Semaphore Operations
mnemonics.content: WAIT DECREMENTS, SIGNAL INCREMENTS - P GOES DOWN (PASSES), V GOES UP (VERHOOG - DUTCH FOR INCREASE)

mnemonics.title: Classical Sync Problems
mnemonics.content: PRODUCER CONSUMER BUFFER, READERS WRITERS DATA, DINING PHILOSOPHERS FORKS - THREE CLASSICS TO KNOW

acronyms.usage: The portion of code where shared resources are accessed and requires synchronization

acronyms.expansion: Proberen (Dutch for test/wait) and Verhogen (Dutch for increment/signal)
acronyms.usage: Standard semaphore operations in European notation
Integer variable with atomic wait() and signal() operations
Hardware instruction used for atomic mutual exclusion implementation
Common queue discipline for semaphore wait queues

visualTips.visualization: Draw two boxes side by side representing processes P0 and P1. Show flags as flags that can be raised (true) or lowered (false). Show turn as an arrow pointing to which process has priority. When both flags up and turn=0, P1 waits.

Think of a semaphore as a counter at a theater entrance. Initial value = number of free seats. wait() = someone enters (decrement), signal() = someone leaves (increment). If counter=0, people must wait in queue.

Draw a circular buffer with IN and OUT pointers. Show producer filling empty slots (moving IN), consumer removing items (moving OUT). When IN catches OUT, buffer is empty. When IN just behind OUT (circular), buffer is full.

Draw five philosophers around a pentagon table with a fork between each pair. Show how picking up forks creates dependency cycles. One solution: odd philosophers pick left then right, even pick right then left.

analogies.analogy: Think of a single bathroom in a house - only one person can use it at a time. The bathroom is the critical section, and the lock ensures mutual exclusion.

A hotel with a certain number of rooms (counting semaphore). When all rooms are full (value=0), new guests (processes) must wait in lobby (queue). When a guest leaves (signal), one waiting guest can check in.
A parking lot with ONE space. The barrier (semaphore) is either up (1=available) or down (0=occupied). Cars (processes) must wait if barrier is down.
Someone repeatedly asking 'Are we there yet?' in a car - consuming attention without making progress, unlike actually sleeping (blocking) until arriving.
A bank teller - only one customer can be served at the counter at a time. The teller (monitor) ensures orderly access to the shared resource (banking services).
